name: Acidgrafix
description: Rule-bending, medium-blending "Instagram grunge". Full of horror vacui, chrome type, oddball textures and self-referentialism.

name: Airbrush Surrealism
description: The uncanny usually-painted 80s illustration aesthetic. Supersaturated colors, juxtaposed objects, chrome and lightning.

name: Austurbane
description: Urbane variety of BDA. Serif fonts, muted colours. Black, beige, copper, bronze and gold. Large amounts of whitespace, minimalism but 'classy', incorporating 'zen' elements in materials used.Product photography, whilst still using similar layouts and perspectives (top-down, isometric) as BDA photography, generally has a more staged feel, with sharper, more dramatic lighting. Plant and branch shadows, prismatic or refractive elements scattering the light. Natural materials, granite and marble. Often rough stones. Fabric.

name: Blob World
description: The wave of motion design and illustration that makes heavy use of strange, elaborate 3D renders to create playful textures, strange/uncanny forms, surrealist compositions, and occasionally unnerving body horror.

images.title: STAFFcirc vol. 2 - AI BOMB VARIATIONS album art (2016)

name: Bubblegum Bling
description: Revival of McBling, heavily influenced by PC Music, named for it's association with some artists in the 'Hyperpop' scene. Largely misattributed as 'y2k' on platforms like Instagram

images.title: LIZ – When I Rule The World (2015)
Hannah Diamond – Soon I Won't See You At All (2017)

name: Cassette Futurism
description: The 70s-and-80s bulky, angular scifi & hardware aesthetic. Synonymous with Japan's bubble. Ex: Syd Mead

name: Contempo Eclectic
description: An eclectic mishmash in the wake of the postmodern design explosion in the early 1980s. The playfulness of New Wave/Pacific Wave graphic design, renewed interest in furniture design experimentation post-Memphis Group/Eurostyle/Neo-Folk, Deco-Luxe, and the commercial popularization of abstract & surrealist art beginning in the late 1970s and early 1980s are all incorporated in this aesthetic.

name: Corporate Gen-X Cyber
description: A short-lived aesthetic, CGXC is composed of a mix of several movements from the late 1980s-onward; from the typographical experimentation of Grunge, the incorporation of computer-generated and manipulated imagery of April Greiman-style graphics, and a watered-down corporate-friendly version of both early 1990s rave flyer graphics and artistic experimentation with computer graphics (eg. Wired Magazine). It seemed to be the go-to style to appear contemporary, especially for companies in industries associated with the booming fields of the era (web, multimedia, telecommunications, biotech, etc.) Certain loose but still somewhat restrained fonts like FF Meta, Template Gothic, Rotis, Din are associated with this aesthetic, modified with Grunge-lite treatments like extended underlines, blur, translucency, displaced/distorted stroke, weights, and sizes. These are paired with images that are sometimes heavily-saturated, warped, blurred (motion blur especially), overlaid on-top of each other with various photoshop effects applied. Recurring colors are 'acid/infrared vision' versions of green, yellow, orange, and purple.

name: Corporate Grunge / Grunge™
description: The ubiquitous 'corporate' grunge look derived from the hype surrounding the Pacific Northwest 'grunge' music scene, the work of graphic designers such as David Carson, Neville Brody & Carlos Segura, and magazines like Raygun & Emigre.

name: Corporate Hippie
description: Appropriation of 'Hippie' culture, motifs & psychedelic graphic styles for corporate applications

name: Corporate Memphis
description: The Generic 10s 'Friendly' Corporate Aesthetic, neo-Memphis, pastel colors, Mondrian influence, corporate appropration of Vaporwave motifs, geometric sans typefaces, Monstera plants, exposed plywood, white walls, Matisse-influenced graphics.

name: Curly Girly
description: Ornate, playful, silly & frilly aesthetics marketed at young girls. The 1990s 'Groovival' aesthetic is a big influence here, along with the popularity of 'Diva' motifs, 2000s celebrity & pop culture, and some of the aspirational wealth/fame popular in McBling.

name: Cyberbougie
description: BDA with a cyber twist. Monospaced fonts, gradients, black backgrounds and a huge emphasis on "sleekness". Seen largely in branding for tech startups and websites.

images.title: ATLAS Institute (University of Colorado) - Lab Icons (2020)

name: Cyberdelia
description: Named for the club in the film, Hackers (1995), this is psychedelic side of Early-Cyber & Silicon Dreams, featuring visual elements like kaleidoscopic palettes, acid colors, fractal forms, and expressive early 3D typography. Can be seen in early rave flyers, media associated with late 80s-early 90s emerging cyberculture.

images.title: PlayStation – Interactive CD Sampler Pack Volume 3 (1996)

name: Deco-Luxe
description: A melange of Art Deco & Streamline Moderne revivals that emerged mainly in the mid 1970s; and peaked in the 1980s and early 1990s. This style differs from the earlier Art Deco revival in the 1970s, 'Disco Deco', as it's more focused on the luxury side of Art Deco & the Roaring '20s (tieing into the general cultural milleu of the go-go 80s).

images.title: Amcoa Decorative Glass and Table Tops Magazine Ad - 1988

name: Decoplex
description: 1990s 'third wave' of art deco revival motifs, influenced more by the 1930s-40s golden age of Hollywood, early air travel, WPA murals, and a playful, exaggerated version of art deco tropes. Less focused on luxury, and less restrained than the art deco revival of the 1980s.

images.title: Batman: Mask of the Phantasm Opening Credits (1993)

name: Diner Kitsch
description: Baby Boomer & Silent Generation revival of various 1940s-1950s cultural iconography, design, and kitsch associated with diner, drive-in's and malt shop culture. Jukeboxes, poodle skirts, 1950s advertising kitsch, neon, chromed-out automobiles, metallic paneling, 'American Graffiti'-style fonts.

images.title: Samantha Fox - Hold On Tight (Music Video) (1986)

name: Disco Deco
description: Art Deco revival in the context of the burgeoning disco scene (Studio 54), filled with bright and vibrant colors, geometric typefaces, some psychedelic imagery

name: Dollar Store Vernacular
description: The perennial ecosystem of "cheap" or "outsider" design. Designs found at dollar stores, knock-off products; eg. designs by those outside of the 'design ecosystem'. Sometimes these include things like: flyers made for a school event with word art, graphics for spammy internet sites/ads, personal websites.

name: Early Cyber
description: April Greiman, Susan Kare, Emigre, Pacific Wave, early rave flyer graphics, The Mind's Eye, harsh pixelated thermographic imagery with warped fonts made newly possible through the proliferation of the first wave of desktop publishing and image manipulation software. (early 80s to ~1993)

images.title: The Central Band Of The Royal Air Force, Barrie Hingley– R.A.F. Brass & Wind Bands  (1992)
Photoshop ad- You can dream in color (1990)
Tangerine Dream ‎– Lily On The Beach  (1989)
William Gibson- Burning Chrome, Neuromancer and Count Zero Japanese covers (1987)

name: Eco-Beige
description: Stripped-down, eco-oriented early 1990s aesthetic. There's something about this style, where it seems like an attempt to distance itself from 1980s excess, and seem more stripped down, unpretentious, eco-friendly, and down to earth. Beige, soft natural tones, twigs, raffia straw, linens, wood, exposed surfaces, textured walls. Not usually super-detailed or ornate, using simple handcrafted forms or just natural forms.

images.title: Save Our Planet soap 'SōP' (year not listed)
What Goes Into Making Paper? - self-promotion (year not listed)
Bio-Molecular Recovery Treatment PR Kit (year not listed)
Noëlle Spa for Beauty and Wellness (ynl)
Noëlle Spa for Beauty and Wellness (ynl)

name: Electroclash
description: Liquid Sky revival, loud and clashing makeup palettes, androgynous glam fashion, revival of some Pacific Punk Wave graphic design.

name: Factory Pomo
description: A design aesthetic that coalesced in the late 1980s, merging the clean, simple, referential forms of Memphis Milano, new visual languages emerging from the CAD technology boom, and revivals of Bauhaus, Russian Constructivism, WPA graphics, and general industrial vernacular from the 1900s-1940s. Coined by Evan Collins.

images.title: C + C Music Factory - Gonna Make You Sweat (1990)
C + C Music Factory - Gonna Make You Sweat Promo (1990)

name: Festival Marketplace
description: A postmodern melange of 1800s-1900s American shopping vernacular, updated in a simplfied, colorful, celebratory form, popularly used in namesake 'Festival Marketplace'-style shopping centers. Serif fonts, dots or other simple shapes between letters, picnic checkerboards, balloons, banners, flags, confetti, diamonds, festival carts, circular logos, remixes of various older American advertising vernacular, sometimes a more fun freeform scribbly or script font to spice things up, simple colorful graphics, sometimes in silhouette. Sibley/Peteet, Steinhilber Deutsch & Gard, Michael Vanderbyl.

images.title: Long Beach Women's History Month Poster (1984)

name: Four Colors
description:
The use of four particularly common colors in tech and general product design throughout the mid-2000s: Electric Lime, Sky Blue, Hot Pink, Tangerine Orange.

Its use was particularly noticeable during the rise of frutiger-aero and superflat-pop, since a driving force in these trends was the personalization of products through color (see: various incarnations of the iPod like the Mini & Nano, as well as cameras like the Kodak DC240i Zoom [1999] and Fujifilm Finepix Z20fd [2008]).

We've observed that this trend has significant ties to consumer product design in Japan, which is especially visible in stores like Daiso. The meaning of the colors has also recently been noted as possibly being a reference to the colors of different seasons in Japan, specifically:
Pink -> Spring
Green -> Summer
Orange -> Autumn
Blue -> Winter

images.title: M & G Stationary Oversized Erasers (Year unknown)

name: Frasurbane
description: Frasurbane (originally titled '90s Urbane) draws from the breakthroughs made by the 1990s Grunge movement, but filtered through the lens of a more conservative and aging Baby Boomer population that was settling down, becoming more wealthy and suburban. Serif fonts abound, accentuated with underlining, italicization, and various weights. These are usually coupled with warm sepia or black-and-white images of 'timeless'/classical representations of basic concepts (sometimes in a surrealist manner), often in soft-focus and featuring heavily-staged product photography. Astrolabes, globes, 'Vitruvian Man' style diagrams, textured paper, Neo-traditional architecture, relatively 'realistic' drawn representations of various traditional objects, plants, and animals (think Victorian-era scientific illustrations). My understanding is that as postmodernism emerged in the 1970s-80s, it started off as brash, 'Memphis' loud, and morphed into a more traditional, classical, and conservative version of itself as it's progenitors aged and the style became subsumed into popular culture and accepted as a capitalistic upper-class signifier. With the economic recession of the early 1990s, this understated form became even more popular, compatible with both the zeitgeist of an aging population (nostalgic photographic treatments, attempts to appear timeless by including 'classic' versions of objects rather than their then-current 1990s iterations) and the surface environmentalism of the era (earthy warm tones, 'eco-friendly' packaging, naturalistic accents like twine, twigs, wooden crates packed with straw, leaves).

name: Frutiger Aero
description:
Name coined by Sofi Xian.

Greenwashed tech aesthetic of the 2000s-2010s. Features tertiary color palettes, stock photos of nature, glassy 3D orbs, bokeh blurs, & auroras. Glossy skeuomorphic GUIs & humanist sans serif type were popular trends (especially in the respective Aqua and Aero design guidelines for OSX and Windows). Soothing, sine-wave based ambient menu music & UI sound design. Personalized product design colorways, especially in tech peripherals like first wave smartphones & MP3 players (via the Four Colors motif). Green architecture & transportation like living rooftops, solar panels, and first wave hybrid EVs. Interior design using white plastic, glass, & bamboo materials. Documentary films & social campaigns on global warming, healthy diets, and agriculture. Common themes of harmony with nature, humanism, & climate consciousness--driven by brands with “eco-friendly” marketing campaigns, typically with the intent of laundering their image and reputations.

name: Gay Nineties Revival
description: Revival of the aesthetics of the 1890s (or "Gay Nineties"), common from the late 60s to late 70s. Common signifiers: Use of ferns as indoor decor, fake Tiffany lamps, use of brass and gold accents with medium-stained oak woods, "old timey" letttering. Color scheme: Brown, orange, and old gold, often with prominent maroon and light lime green accents. Prominent designers: Herb Lubalin.

images.title: The One and Only Genuine Original Family Band (Poster) (1968)
Clyde's Bar at Tysons Corner (Year Unknown)
Jimmy Buffett ‎- "Changes In Latitudes, Changes In Attitudes" Album Art (1977)

name: Gen X Soft Club
description: Contrary to contemporaneous Y2K Aesthetic, and also McBling style of the early 2000s, Gen X Y/AC models in editorial fashion or artist marketing were much more "natural". You will see tons of nude lipstick, hair parted down the middle or natural textured hair, natural leathers, knee high boots, duster jackets, natural and muted colors - especially greens, beiges, tans, greys, and black. Design and fashion was moving from 60s revival into early 70s. Retro-futurism was Y2K Aesthetic, but Gen X YAC was more terrestrial. Its futurism was a mix of sterile and organic. McBling was a full blown disco revival, but here, we just see hints of the 70s. Depictions of city life through a colonialist lens. First wave gentrification. Urban life influenced graphic design, such as in the use of Helvetica as inspired by the NYC subway signs. The rise of a minimalist design revival.

images.title: Spiritualized® ‎– Ladies And Gentlemen We Are Floating In Space (1997)
Spiritualized – Ladies and Gentlemen We Are Floating in Space Blister Pack (1997)
Bang On A Can – Music For Airports - Brian Eno (1998)

name: Gen-X Home
description: The softer home marketing segment version of CGXC

images.title: Home Idea Notebook: A Design Guide for Every Room in the House (2001)
'Pop Art' collection by Massimo Losa Ghini for Roche Bobois (ynl)

name: Genericana
description: 1920s - 1950s packaging, sign painting fonts, references to American vernacular and skeuomorphisms with negative space to denote premium feel & materiality

images.title: The Devil Makes Three - I'm A Stranger Here (2015)
Ben Miller Band - Choke Cherry Tree (2018)

name: Geo-Boho
description: Early-Mid 10s Tumblr Hipster Aesthetic. Triangles, geometric line motifs, mandalas, condensed hand-written block type, watercolours, Native American cultural appropriation, washed out colours, faux-analogue Instagram filtered imagery, galaxies, tapestries 'oneness'/spiritual themes

images.title: "Earthbound Trading Company" Eureka Springs, Arkansas (Year Unknown)

name: Global Village Coffeehouse
description: A network of aesthetics emerging in the late 1980s, some an evolution of prog-punk-zolo Memphis-y squiggles, Keith Haring, woodcut revival, a "return-to-the-natural" handrawing movement, reaction against the computer aided design boom, late 80s environmentalism revival, etc. Still very postmodern in the sense of appropriating seemingly endless prior artistic movements, mainly for commercial/corporate purposes. Peaks in the mid 1990s, falling out of favor later on as the pendulum swung back to the minimalism/tech/clean vibes of Y2K & Gen X/YAC. It's very wide-ranging and could be split into many sub-groups, but this format seems to work better. Common motifs include: woodcuts, 'tribal/ancient imagery and iconography', moons, suns, spirals, hands, eyes, stars, simple styled flowing/curvy figures, 'aroma swirls', coffee cups, natural elements like trees/waves/landscapes, earth tones, hand-drawn look, 'airbrushed dirty look', the earth/globe, hearts, colorful gradated backgrounds, rough irregular borders & lines.

name: Googie Kitsch
description: Revival of the playful side of mid-century modern design (eg. colorful George Nelson clocks, boomerang tables), Googie motifs, general 1950's advertising vernacular & pop culture kitsch. Emerged in hip Pacific Punk Wave design circles mainly in the late 1970s, peaking in various forms & fields throughout the late 20th century, until petering out by the early 2000s. The slightly later 'lounge' revival (late 1980s/early 1990s) while having overlapping elements, could be seen as a distinct aesthetic since it pulls from some different sources (1960s bachelor pad style, exotica & tiki culture, animal prints). Overlaps with Diner Kitsch, though that aesthetic mainly pulls from older American diners from the 1930s-1950s built in the Streamline Moderne style.

name: Graffiti Pop
description: First wave of corporate appropriation of Graffiti culture. Typically includes urban streetscapes & iconography contemporary to the time period (eg. trash cans, brick walls, chain link fencing, street lamps, sidewalks, industrial elements, general 'urban decay'). It's also associated with that eras' portrayal of street, hip hop, & punk culture.

images.title: Tina Weymouth and Grandmaster Flash Photograph (1981)
Safire - Boy I've Been Told (Official Music Video) (1988)

name: Groovival
description: The 1990s rehashing of 1960s "hippie" / "flower power" aesthetics, as both a nostalgia play to boomers and an attempt to entice kids with bright psychedelic shapes and colors.

images.title: Deee-Lite - Groove Is In The Heart (1990)
Deee-Lite – Groove Is In The Heart (1990)
Deee-Lite – Groove Is In The Heart (1990)
Saint Etienne - You're In A Bad Way (1993)

name: Hexatron
description: The Sci-Fi Angular Future Aesthetic of the 10s - Hexagons, Neon Blue, Holograms, Terminal-inspired UIs, cyberpunk-adjacent visuals. Angular technology with heavy ornamentation, sometimes white and shiny like with #frutiger-aero but generally less clean, less curved.

name: Hipness Purgatory
description: Things associated with the "hipster" movement of the late Bush & early-mid Obama years, both in the first wave and the backlash. Characterized by loose drawings, feeling handcrafted with everyday objects, an ironic tone, and a "twee" retro atmosphere.

images.title: Junior Senior ‎- "Hey Hey My My Yo Yo" Album Art (2005)
The Mountain Goats - This Year (Official Music Video) (circa 2005)
"The Royal Tenenbaums" Criterion Collection Box Art (2001)

name: Indie Sleaze
description:
Name coined by Dalia Barillaro.

Post-Electroclash clubbing movement. Candid, low fidelity photography of partygoers at clubs and afterparties. Millennial street fashion (American Apparel, Converse shoes, ironic graphic T-shirts, hoodies, skinny jeans, shutter shades). Musically embodied by the MySpace music scene, bloghaus, and other attempts at combining electro-house with pop, rock, and hip-hop. References 70s nostalgia via disco samples, extravagant costumes, and supergraphic typography. Themes of hedonistic excess and clubbing as an escape from daily stress (especially in the context of the economic crash of 08’).

images.title: Two Door Cinema Club - "Tourist History" Album Art (2011)
New Club Kids: London Party Fashion in the Noughties (2011)
Empire Of The Sun - Walking On A Dream (Official Music Video) (2009)
Lady Gaga - Just Dance ft. Colby O'Donis (Official Music Video) ft. Colby O'Donis

name: Indiecraft
description: The hipster aesthetic of kooky "charming" overauthentic visuals, handcrafted imagery, and music videos with puppets in them.

images.title: They Might Be Giants – Electric Car (2009)
They Might Be Giants – I'm Impressed (2007)
The Bird and the Bee – The Bird and the Bee (2006)

name: Industrial Americana
description: Public domain clip art from the 40s-60s as vernacular, popularized by Charles S. Anderson, French Paper Co. Manifested in interior & furniture design as subdued 'industrial' color palettes, retro-inspired industrial vernacular eg. rusted steel beams, heavy metals textures, mesh, concrete, brick etc.

name: Industrial Gothic
description: Dark, moody industrial imagery of the late 80s and 90s. Wires, cabling, piping. Peeling paint, grunge-precursor aspects, strange dark machines, often monochrome and moodily lit. Darker variant of Factory Pomo and 80s-DIY.

images.title: Prodigy - The Dirtchamber Sessions Volume One (1999)

name: Internet Awesomesauce
description: ‘Awesome’ or ‘Epic’ visuals from the Early 10s, still in places up to the end of the decade. Rainbows, cats, unicorns, galaxies and space, lasers, junk food and 80s airbrush motifs, used ironically. Meme aspects. ‘Random’ and absurd compositions and juxtapositions.

images.title: Shawn Wasabi - Mac 'n' Cheese cover art

name: Jumbled Font
description: Potentially emerging from the Pacific Punk Wave movement, and influential/contemporaneous with Grunge, this style is specific to typography. Stretched letters in every direction, jumbled, playful mixing of fonts & colors, objects substituted for letters, calligrams, etc. Note this is distinct from the more dirty, grungy 'ransom note collage' look.

images.title: Cincinnati Mills Mall - Information Desk Sign (2002)
Urban Cookie Collective - High on a Happy Vibe (1994)
Nice Up Dancee: The RAS Tapes 2 (1991)

name: Laser Grid
description: The 80's high-tech design style that later became a main component in the vaporwave, synthwave, etc. aesthetics of the early 2010s. Precedes PW/EC style which is mainly prevalent in the late 1980s. Laser grids, glass blocks, sleek metallic surfaces, reflective glass (think state-of-the-art 80's corporate office park) glowing neon in a high-tech application (sometimes referencing art deco), chromed-out airbrush effects, robots, simple pomo shapes and compositions.

name: Live Laugh Love
description: Wood grain, brush script fonts, and snarky slogans about how often you drink wine. Still going strong in suburban homes worldwide.

images.title: Sophia - Free Handlettered Brush Script Font (2015)
Lunar Abundance: Cultivating Joy, Peace, and Purpose Using the Phases of the Moon (2018)

name: McBling
description: The period from approx. 2000 to 2008, a hard shift away from the minimalist futurism of the Y2K era towards maximalist remixes of mainly 1970s and early 1980s trends & motifs. Aspirational luxury in all forms, tattoo motifs, oversized designer glasses, celebutantes, designer ripped jeans, pink hummers, bedazzled electronics, fashion phones, McMansions, nihilistic & hedonistic overtones, overly-flourishy vector art, grunge effects applied to 'gothic' fonts, reality tv shows, Paris Hilton, tuscan-inspired-everything in home furnishings, luxury trips around the world, excessive overbuilding of homes (global housing bubble), designer dogs, dubai, bling, spinning rims, trucker hats, von dutch, ed hardy, spray tans, tracksuits, Juicy Couture, american flag inspired clothing post-9/11.

images.title: The Pussycat Dolls - "I Don't Need a Man" Single Cover (2006)
Invitation for Paris Hilton's 22nd Birthday Party (2003)
Gwen Stefani - "Love. Angel. Music. Baby." Album Art (2004)

name: Memphis Jr.
description: The children's version of Memphis-Milano style postmodernism, popular from the late 1980s to the mid 1990s. It was gradually replaced by other kid-centric aesthetics like Utopian Scholastic & Wonka Pomo.

images.title: "Chasing the Rainbow" Segment from Elroy's Toy (1995)

name: Memphis-Milano
description: Designs of Memphis Group, Studio Alchimia, and those inspired by their geometric, colorful designs across the world in the 1980s.

name: Metalheart
description: Centers around the 'Metalheart / Transformer / Depthcore' aesthetic popular from around 1998-2004. It's named after the Metalheart book by Andreas Lindholm (2001). Pioneered by design firms like the Attik, Brainreactor, Gmunk, etc. in the late 1990s, it peaked & became oversaturated by the early 2000s, then essentially vanished in the late 2000s, morphing into McBling (basically Design Student's Orgasm on TVTropes) vector graphics, and replaced by more hand-drawn Hipness Purgatory 'indie twee' design. Many of these are collected from the piecemeal archives of old design sites like depthcore, archived sections of designers portfolios, deviantart and customize.org, and some scans I've taken from the Metalheart books, Plazm Magazine, and various design collection books from the era.

images.title: Utada - You Make Me Want To Be A Man (2004)

name: Mid Century Medieval
description: The very kitschy revival of Medieval and Renaissance motifs & cultural touchstones in the context of the 1950s-on economic boom. Expressed in the emergence of 'Renaissance Faires', Tudor-style and Fairytale suburban architecture, Sword-and-Sorcery movies, Steak & Ale restaurant chain, themed hotels. Additionally some examples are from the Hippie variant of this revival, in the 1960s-70s.

name: Millennium Disco
description: The slick, technologically-infused gen-X disco revival. Bauhaus typography and 70s-throwback photography are common.

images.title: Hardkandy ‎– How Do You Do Nothing? (2002)
Junior Senior ‎– D-D-Don't Don't Stop The Beat  (2002)

name: Mission School
description: Mainly muted colors, faux-weathered textures and reappropriation of midcentury / Americana imagery. Common mediums include collage and painting. This style has been quite difficult to pin down, and the gallery contains some examples that may be re-classified at some future point, along with some that seem to have influenced the style (eg. Basquiat).

images.title: You should all know something before we start on the second half of today's session (2002)
They are all with the sunlight of the spirit (2002)

name: Neo-Vectorheart
description: A half-revival of circa-2000 vectorheart incorporated with maximalist "gamer-grunge". All about 45-degree angles, punchy colors, and contrast.

images.title: Audeka - Dark Energy / Direct Injection - Single

name: Neo-Y2K
description: Revival of Y2K Aesthetic, with more emphasis on Eastern graphic design influences, saturated colors, and a convergence of Metalheart forms.

images.title: Kit Major - Lost in My Head (2019)

name: Neoclassical Pomo
description: Revival of Neoclassical architecture (reintroduction of Roman/Greek features), as one of the cornerstones of Postmodern architecture and design.

name: Neon Ooze
description: The ubiquitous neon ooze motif that proliferated in kids (and sometimes adult) media in the 1980s & 1990s. Mad scientists, toxic waste, sludge, slime, ooze, blobs, globs, and general gross-out content. Probably tied to some general societal anxiety about the environment in the 1980s. Note: Name sourced from the Facebook group, 'Neon Ooze/Surf Shack' created by Karl Kraft. Some examples are also sourced from that group, and some are from our internal CARI research.

images.title: Devo Presents Adventures of the Smart Patrol Magazine Ad (1996)
Burger King "Choose the Ooze!" Ad Campaign (2001-2002)

name: New Wave Tropical
description: Vibrant, colorful, tropical & rainforest imagery influenced by the wild & freeform visual experimentation of Pacific Punk Wave. There was also renewed interest, and some appropriation of the visual motifs of African, Caribbean & Latin cultures. A specific motif we've termed 'Colorful Cutout' is core to this aesthetic: simple, sometimes chunky irregular color shapes with that 'construction paper collage' look.

images.title: "Croc's Bar and Grill Boca Raton" Menu (Year Unknown)

name: Nouveau Organic
description: Revival of Gaudi-Catalan Modernisme, Art Nouveau, early-mid 20th century organic architecture, under-the-sea vibes (sometimes), generally a naturalistic style prevalent in the very late 80s and 90s. Jordan Mozer, Barbara Lazaroff, Ayala Serfaty, and Javier Senosiain are some of the main names.

name: Nu-Brutalism
description: Neo-Swiss/International Style of design: often with heavily contrasting colors, various Grotesque typefaces, and emphasis on structure of typography.

name: Pacific Punk Wave
description: Graphic style that emerged from the Punk, Pacific Wave, Swiss New Wave, and Italian radical design movements of the 1970s and 80s. There's also some influence of the '1950s diner/googie/populuxe' aesthetic sometimes, epitomized by the popularity of 'Heaven', a novelty kitsch store in LA. These styles were very influential on the '80s look' as it's commonly known in the 2010s revival.

images.title: WET: The Magazine of Gourmet Bathing, Sept/Oct '78 Issue (1978)

name: Paperback Chic
description: Natural, soft colors, didone or retro display typefaces and airy composition style. Sometimes called "Chobanicore" in reference to the 2017 Chobani yogurt rebrand by Will Mac

name: Pastel Southwestern
description: 1980s iteration of 'southwestern' styles fused with the prevailing pastel color palette of the era. Sits between the earlier 1970s dark toned Southwestern-Mission style (eg. the original Taco Bell design), and the later 1990s-2000s earthy-toned Tuscan style. Pastel tones, plush furnishings, light wood accents, adobe accents, the emergence of the Kokopelli, geometric patterns derived from Indigenous-peoples art.

name: Pixelscape
description: Ubiquitous on the early web. The often-isometric aesthetic of lush, elaborate pixel art and similar motifs in web design and illustration.

images.title: Captain Planet and the Robots of Zarm (2004)

name: Polychrome
description: Ink in water, smoke, liquids, oils, paint explosions, blurring, melding colours. Usually a wide color palette.

name: Pomo Faux Ruins
description: Intentionally distressed and crumbling structures as an architecture storytelling element (ex. Best stores). Ranges from 'contemporary ruins', eg. then-current spaces with distressed, crumbling, ripped, or decayed elements, to 'ancient ruins', which specifically reference motifs from Classical antiquity.

images.title: Goldi Shoe Store at Woodfield Mall (1986)
Goldi Shoe Store at Woodfield Mall (1986)
Goldi Shoe Store at Woodfield Mall (1986)
Goldi Shoe Store at Woodfield Mall (1986)

name: Preschool Pop
description: Crayons, pencils, the classic little kid drawing style (stick figures, house, sun, tree, etc.), oversized versions of toys, esp. alphabet blocks.

images.title: Crayola Kids Store at Woodfield Mall, Schaumburg IL (1992)
Crayola Kids Store at Woodfield Mall, Schaumburg IL (1992)
Crayola Kids Store at Woodfield Mall, Schaumburg IL (1992)
The Toy Box at Regal Cinemas Funscape (YNL)
Texas Scottish Rite Hospital for Children (Pre-1999)
Texas Scottish Rite Hospital for Children (Pre-1999)

name: Pulp Fantasy
description: The "70s scifi" aesthetic of dreamy, surreal paintings and imaginative illustration. Examples show up in both Sci-Fi & Fantasy genres. Seen often used in visuals for prog rock.

images.title: Harry Harrison - The Stainless Steel Rat

name: Rad Dog / Neon Surf
description: Neon paint splatters, hypercolors, tribal motifs, and "radical" surf / skate imagery. Pioneered by edgy, mold-breaking teen-marketed apparel and equipment brands. Note: Most images, along with the term, are sourced & curated from the Facebook group, Neon Ooze/Surf Shack, created by Karl Kraft.

name: Radical Surrealism
description: Emerged in the very late 1960s and peaked in the 1970s, when the Pop Art movement was influencing graphic, product, furniture, and interior design. The main proponents of this style came out of the Italian radical design movement; groups like Studio Alchimia and Memphis Milano to breakout in the 1980s. 'Think Big', a chain selling oversized 'Pop' object versions of everyday household items, also emerged from this zeitgeist.

name: Renaissance Revival
description: 1990s and millennium ren faire, antique gothic, Shakespearean/Elizabethan, Mediaeval, Renaissance Greco-Roman art interpretation, magick, and faerie tale cultural revival. Most often mixed with obvious anachronisms. Runway shows inspired by Renaissance fashion; a reprinting of Shakespeare that utilizes Elizabethan style design elements; 90s/millennium formal attire with faerie tale inspiration.

images.title: Loreena McKennitt - The Book of Secrets (1997)
Loreena McKennitt - The Mask And Mirror (1994)
Lilith Fair (A Celebration Of Women In Music) (1998)
Sixpence None The Richer - Sixpence None The Richer (1997)

name: Silicon Dreams
description: The surrealist aesthetics of the early days of 3D animation.

images.title: Neutral Milk Hotel - In the Aeroplane Over the Sea

name: Sportsbrut
description: Brutalist, grungy, and powerful, Sportsbrut uses simple, coarse grids, high-impact typography and grunge elements to convey action, excitement, and energy.

name: Superflat Pop
description: The commercial side to Japan's Superflat art movement, with artists like Takashi Murakami's bright and poppy graphics influencing fashion design, motion graphics, and in animation.

images.title: Takashi Murakami For Louis Vuitton - Superflat First Love (2009)

name: Supergraphic Ultramodern
description: Plastics, curves, plush materials, rainbows, inflatables, shiny metals, fibreglass, prefab everything, supergraphics galore, pleasure furniture, Helvetica, icons. This style was a huge influence on Y2K aesthetic in the 1990s.

name: Synthwave
description: The tongue-in-cheek oversaturated 1980s-revival aesthetic also sometimes called "Outrun" or "Retrowave". Heavy use of neon colors, airbrush-style imagery and laser grids.

images.title: Tupperware Remix Party- The Device EP (2012)

name: Teenpunk
description: The millennial and gen-Z iteration of edgy grunge aesthetics, associated closely with "pop punk" and the store Hot Topic.

images.title: Avril Lavigne – The Best Damn Thing (2007)

name: Themed Spaces
description: Highly stylized exterior & interior spaces with their own distinct language. May also have specially-designed collateral, music, clothing for the total immersive package. While these existed prior to the 1990s (and continue to exist in some forms after), they peaked in this era of 'experience economy' hype.

name: Ultramodern Revival
description: Very late 90s - early 2000s revival of Supergraphic Ultramodern. Supergraphics, bright color palettes, and sleek forms mix with Millennium Disco kitsch, evolving from the Gen-X Soft Club nostalgia for the 1970s.

images.title: Yoshinori Sunahara – Pan Am The Sound of ‘70s (1998/1999)
Pizzicato Five ‎– The Fifth Release From Matador (2000)

name: UrBling
description: Urban-McBling' but not as heavy with the Bling aspect, though similar in the maximalism, visual excess, and some influences. Silhouetted streetlights, power lines, buildings, highways, grungy effects, wings, crests, flames, tribal tattoo-looking motifs, paint splatters/drips, and sepia toned/reds yellows orange. Blacks and Greys, slab san-serifs, graffiti and stencil motifs & fonts, gothic fonts. Motifs derived from 'Kustom Kulture' also seem to play a part in this style. Tied the relatively strict gender roles in marketing and wider culture at the time, as this could be seen as the 'masculinized' version of McBling.

images.title: Sony PSP Squirrels "Hells Yeah" Ad Campaign (Year Unknown)
Tony Thomas ‎- "21st Century Dub" Album Art (2003)
Universal Studios Florida "Hollywood Rip Ride Rockit" Concept Sketch (2009)
EA Criterion Games "Burnout Paradise" Main Menu (2009)

name: Utopian Scholastic
description:
In some ways this aesthetic is the 'kids version' of Frasurbane, with an emphasis on scholastic endeavors, 'edutainment', and an 'end-of-history' approach to learning. It uses the same classical and 'timeless' typography and imagery with Frasurbane, adding in the aesthetic of 'stock photography collage' depicting the most basic and identifiable depictions of forms and concepts for quick comprehension. This reliance on this style of collage is predicated on two factors in the era; the proliferation of stock photography collections, and the development of desktop publishing & graphics-editing software in the 1980s.

Note: The term 'Utopian Scholastic', was derived from the Facebook group of it's origin, 'Utopian Scholastic Designs from a pre-9/11 World', created by Suze Huldt. Some images shown here are sourced & curated from that group, and some are CARI member scans & finds.

name: Vaporwave
description: The post-internet movement, closely tied with the music genre, of 1980s and '90s nostalgia warped by postmodern irony.

images.title: FLOOR BABA- It Dont Make Sense (2016)
Alex Israel at the Huntington Library (2015-2016)

name: Vector Minimalism
description: This was a minimalistic, quietish era, defined by the decrease in Skeumorphism of this era towards a "flat" aesthetic that in manys was heavily referential of Mid-Century Modernism. It started in 2012, became the dominant aesthetic of silicon valley in mid-2013, and faded out to some extent by the late 2010s. Examples: "Flat" anything; paring down the naturalistic tendencies of Hipness Purgatory and replacing them with an angularity that both recalls Mid-century designs and anticipates developments in Bougie Design Aesthetics; iOS 7; geometric sans serif fonts, particularly combined with Bodoni or Didot-esque bold serif display fonts (common 2015-present)

name: Vectorheart
description: Pioneered by firms like Bionic Systems and The Designers' Republic, vectorheart embodies the blazing speed of y2k attitudes with futuristic forms and high-contrast typography that borrows from both Swiss modernism and utilitarian industrial design.

images.title: Teebee - Through the Eyes of A Scorpion (2001)
Bright Child activity centre - Santa Monica, CA (1998)
Bright Child activity centre - Santa Monica, CA (1998)
Bright Child activity centre - Santa Monica, CA (1998)

name: Whimsicraft
description: Part of the folk art and furniture revival of the 1980s, along with a new interest in collage, craft, repurposing industrial/found objects. It's part of the 'down to earth', folksy, colorful 'art fair' atmosphere of the time period. Formerly called 'DIY Handmade Folk Artsy 80s-90s', renamed in Sept 2021.

images.title: Toad The Wet Sprocket ‎- "Fear" Album Art (1991)
Out Of The Box (Opening Theme Song) (1998)
Harriet the Spy - Windchime Garden Clip (1996)

name: Whimsigothic
description: This aesthetic was contemporaneous with the peak popularity of gothic-inspired pop/rock music, Tim Burton, and the graphic design work of Margo Chase, Vrontikis, and a bit of Vaughan Oliver. Very popular in the fields of furniture, product, and interior design. Note: This aesthetic was originally titled 'Whimsical Mystical Gothic Celestial'.

name: Y2K Aesthetic
description: A time when the future was tight leather pants, silver eyeshadow, shiny clothing, oakleys, gradients, and blobby electronics. Also known as 'Y2K Futurism'

name: Zen-X
description: Popular with Gen-X and some Baby Boomers; this aesthetic emerged in the early 1990s, part of the 'natural-organic' shift away from 1980s excess and a resurgent wave of environmentalism (eg. Sting in the rainforest, the corporate eco-responsibility trend made famous by companies like The Body Shop, the Nature Company, etc.). This was combined with a trend towards minimalism and renewed interest in Japanese architecture and design concepts.

images.title: Melanie C - Never Be The Same Again (2000)
Anggun - A Rose In the wind (1998)
